bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes Russian culpability and escalation, framing Moscow as "aiding" Iran and using phrases like "help Tehran strike" and "comprehensive effort"; right-leaning pieces stress security threat, invoke "attack" and "dragging a nuclear rival into the war," and name "American warships" and " aircraft " to raise urgency.

The left uses accusatory language while the right deploys militarized, alarmist diction; dividing lines include Lavrov's rhetoric, center caveats about intelligence limits and White House "downplayed" response, and technical details .
Both sides agree U.S. officials reported shared targeting data, revealing a split between security and accountability priorities.
summary: On Mar 6, 2026, two U.S. officials told the AP that Russia provided Iran with information that could help Tehran strike American warships and aircraft.
Moscow tightened ties as it sought missiles and drones for its war in Ukraine, while Tehran supplied Shahed drones used in retaliatory Gulf attacks.
Space-Based tracking showed Moscow's about 110 military satellites and Iran's around 13 enable imagery and target monitoring of the USS Gerald R. Ford.

The White House downplayed reports of Russia sharing intelligence with Iran, while US Central Command's Admiral Brad Cooper said `This is unacceptable and will not go unanswered` amid Iranian drone and missile strikes in Bahrain.
Analysts warn the conflict has slowed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and raised oil prices, while the UN refugee agency calls the situation a major humanitarian emergency.
title: Source Icon

summary: U.S. President Donald Trump stated that the only acceptable outcome for ending hostilities with Iran is 'unconditional surrender.'
Trump emphasized that after Iran's capitulation, the U.S. and allies would help make Iran 'economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before.'
He reiterated that there will be no deal with Iran except for 'unconditional surrender.'
Trump introduced a new slogan, 'MAKE IRAN GREAT AGAIN ,' paralleling his previous political branding.
title: Trump Says There Will Be No Deal with Iran Except 'Unconditional Surrender'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the BLS numbers as evidence of economic strain and policy failure—using phrases like "under strain," "destroyed," and "flashing red"—while right-leaning pieces stress how the miss complicates political standing and point to external causes, using words such as "sputtering," "dismal," or "jobs shock.

Pivotal dividing lines are attribution to presidential policy versus geopolitical/inflationary causes and whether one-off factors explain the drop; center outlets emphasize wage, participation and seasonality nuance de-emphasized by both sides, yet all note the surprise may alter Fed timing.
summary: On March 6, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported the United States economy shed 92,000 jobs in February, and the unemployment rate rose to 4.4%.
Broader pressures such as tariffs, AI adoption, and high interest rates weighed on hiring, with construction losing 11,000 jobs last month and healthcare shedding 28,000 after a strike.

The Labor Department's revisions showed private payroll measures dipped by 86,000 in February, trimmed 69,000 jobs from December and January payroll revisions, and average hourly earnings rose 15 cents to $37.32.
Policymakers and markets reacted: the report could alter the Federal Reserve 's calculus ahead of its March 17 meeting as market futures fell and crude oil spiked amid the Iran conflict.
The report deepens a recent trend showing the three-month moving average fell to 6,000, with the breakeven hiring rate near zero to 50,000 jobs a month.
title: U.S. payrolls unexpectedly fell by 92,000 in February; unemployment rate rises to 4.4%

bias: Left outlets frame the $200–$220 million ad buys as a "propaganda blitz," stressing alleged no-bid awards, an 8‑day‑old firm and "handpicked" contracts, using charged verbs like "grilled" and "battered" to imply misconduct; right coverage emphasizes DHS claims—2.2M "self-deportations" and "saved taxpayers $39B"—portraying the campaign as effective and depicting Senate attacks as overblown with words like "savages"; center coverage focuses on procurement questions and optics rather than extremes.

Key divides are efficacy metrics versus contracting irregularities and the alleged ouster narrative; both sides agree the spending, ties to operatives, and congressional scrutiny matter, reflecting deeper splits over immigration, patronage, and fiscal priorities.
summary: This past week, NBC News reported that Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem handpicked contractors for an ICE recruitment campaign, according to internal communications and three administration officials, while a DHS spokesperson said decisions were made by the ICE Director’s office.

To meet recruitment targets, the campaign prioritized speed over an open bidding process, as DHS invoked a national emergency allowing avoidance of competitive bidding to hire 10,000 new ICE officers.

Federal records list People Who Think and Safe America Media, LLC as primary awardees, with Safe America Media incorporated days before winning $143 million and The Strategy Group subcontracted $226,000.

At recent hearings, lawmakers from both parties pressed Noem about the contracts, and President Donald Trump announced she will leave office March 31, with Markwayne Mullin, R-Okla., named as replacement.

Transparency concerns grew because subcontractors are not required to be disclosed, complicating oversight, and Sen. John Kennedy warned "It troubles me that a fifth to a quarter-billion dollars was spent amid budget fights".
title: Noem Bypassed Bidding on $100M ICE Contracts, NBC Reports

bias: Left coverage foregrounds institutional accountability—words like "ethics probe," "affair," "embattled," and "pressure campaign" frame Gonzales as pushed out—while right coverage favors sensational and strategic frames, using "sex suicide scandal," spotlighting party leaders' calls, saying his exit "clears the way" and profiling his successor; center pieces quote his "mistake"/"lapse in judgment" and use more restrained language.
A key divide is graphic emphasis on the aide dousing herself in gasoline and later dying by suicide and attention to the admission venue and successor's politics .
Both sides report the withdrawal, admission, and ethics inquiry.
summary: Thursday, Gonzales announced on X he would not seek re-election, stating, 'After deep reflection and with the support of my loving family, I have decided not to seek re-election while serving out this Congress,' Gonzales said.

The House Ethics Committee opened an investigation into whether Gonzales "engaged in sexual misconduct" earlier this week, while top House Republican leaders urged him to withdraw amid public release of explicit texts from May 2024 as early voting unfolded.

Regina Ann Santos-Aviles died in September 2025 in Uvalde, Texas, and the medical examiner ruled her death a suicide; Gonzales said he had "absolutely nothing to do with her tragic passing" and took responsibility for his mistake.

Gonzales's departure clears the path for Brandon Herrera, while the 218-214 Republican congressional majority makes Gonzales's decision to remain crucial, and House GOP leadership including Speaker Mike Johnson urged the Ethics Committee to act expeditiously.

The Ethics Committee's jurisdiction is limited to sitting lawmakers, and the Office of Congressional Conduct completed its inquiry within 60 days of Gonzales's primary, affecting result publication timing.
title: GOP Rep. Tony Gonzales drops re-election bid amid ethics probe into his affair with a staffer

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes instability and judgment, framing the exit with terms like "controversial," "tumultuous tenure," and "for a second time," portraying the departure as evidence of broader trouble under the administration; right-leaning pieces de-emphasize policy detail and instead foreground the announcement and labels like "embattled" and references to " Trump administration " to personalize the drama.

Center coverage sticks closer to procedural background—ouster/reinstatement and specific vaccine or rare-disease policy disputes—while both sides agree on the April departure and that Prasad’s tenure drew contention, reflecting deeper ideological splits over regulatory politics.
summary: The FDA confirmed Vinay Prasad, a top official overseeing vaccines and gene therapies, will depart in April and has not named a successor.

On 2026-03-06, the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research increased scrutiny and rejected at least five cell and gene therapies, with a recent high-profile rejection of a rare-blood-cancer drug, amid disputes with drugmakers, and the Health and Human Services Department criticized an experimental Huntington's treatment the day before.
Sources describe management actions that reshaped CBER's internal operations, including firing staff and demanding calendar visibility from California, after a brief ouster and rehiring in July.

Prasad's actions produced immediate regulatory consequences, including a White House-intervened review after he refused to review a Moderna flu vaccine, and control of vaccine surveillance shifted from career staff.

Amid a broader shift under Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., Prasad's exit signals wider policy implications as the FDA adopts a more vaccine-skeptical approach; this is a developing story STAT will update.
title: FDA vaccines chief Vinay Prasad to step down in April

bias: Left-Leaning pieces frame the 9-1 result as a straightforward, schedule-focused win—using language like "easily handled" and noting an off day before Mexico—while right-leaning coverage foregrounds drama and individual heroics, highlighting a "dazzling debut" for Clay Holmes, a "nervy start," and a decisive "five‑run fifth" to portray a comeback and "rout.
Center outlets tilt toward stats and commerce, emphasizing box scores and "Best NJ sportsbook promos.
The pivotal divides are the pitching debut and the fifth-inning momentum , even as all sides agree on Schwarber’s homer, Henderson’s four hits and the 9-1 score.
summary: On Saturday, March 7, Team USA routed Great Britain 9-1 in Pool B play in Houston, improving to 2-0 before facing Mexico on Monday, March 9.
Fueled by patient hitting, the U.S. lineup drew seventeen walks and Kyle Schwarber's two‑run homer shifted momentum in the fifth inning.
Tarik Skubal opened for the U.S., striking out five with two hits allowed, while Clay Holmes threw three shutout innings with six strikeouts and Gunnar Henderson went 4-for-5.
The victory moved the U.S. to 2-0, giving Team USA momentum in Pool B before facing the Mexico national baseball team on March 9, 2026 at 8:00 p.m. ET with quarterfinal seeding at stake.

Looking to the roster picture, Team USA arrived with a loaded, MLB-heavy roster, and Skenes said, `Out of all the jerseys I've had over the past few years, the USA. one is the one I'm most proud of wearing.
title: World Baseball Classic: Team USA cruises to another win vs. Great Britain

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes Hegseth’s rhetoric itself, framing the speech as "cringey," "terrifying," and "gaslighting," portraying it as theatrical and potentially dangerous, while right-leaning coverage reframes the story as a media scandal, accusing the broadcaster of "mistranslating" "regime" into "people" and using charged tags like "Caught," "Doctoring," and "Busted" to imply institutional bias and defend Hegseth .

The pivotal split is the lexical swap and whether scrutiny targets policy rhetoric or perceived media malpractice; both sides agree the speech was politically consequential, exposing deeper ideological and geopolitical fault lines.
summary: BBC Persian mistranslated US Secretary Pete Hegseth's speech on the Iran conflict, changing 'regime' to 'people,' which implied the US targeted Iranian civilians rather than the regime.
The mistranslation sparked criticism from Iranians and pro-Israel media, accusing BBC Persian of bias and damaging its credibility.
BBC admitted the error was a human mistake during live simultaneous translation and issued corrections on air and social media.
This incident follows a $10 billion defamation lawsuit by President Donald Trump against the BBC for allegedly misleading edits of his 2021 Capitol speech.
title: BBC Persian Mistranslates Hegseth's Speech on Iran War

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as corrective and pro-labor, spotlighting "voids," "job cuts," and labels like "illegally elevated" or "right-wing judge," often using moralized, partisan diction and quoting phrases such as "activist rulings"; center outlets emphasize legal mechanics with terms like "violated federal law," "invalidating" and "sweeping" and note broader operational impacts, adopting a clinical tone.
Right-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized in this sample.

Pivotal dividing lines—the judge's ideological background and the subject's vow to appeal—drive the sharpest partisan reads; both sides agree the judge voided 2025 actions, revealing a clash over institutional authority versus labor and political legitimacy.
summary: Saturday's ruling by Judge Lamberth determined Kari Lake was not legally eligible to serve as acting CEO of the U.S. Agency for Global Media and declared her actions `null and void`, the two-page decision was tweeted March 7, 2026.
Judge Lamberth found the appointment violated the Federal Vacancies Reform Act and the Appointments Clause because Lake was neither confirmed nor serving in an office subject to advice and consent.

Actions cited by the court included layoffs of about 1,400 VOA staffers and contractors, administrative leaves, and blocking Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty from broadcasting into Iran using a USAGM transmitter in Kuwait.

The ruling leaves major personnel moves without legal force, complicating VOA operations as the court held Lake's actions cannot be ratified and free speech advocates denounced the cuts while the White House has not commented.
Amid allegations of systematic dismantling, the court's ruling deems Lake's claim she `assumed the role of Acting CEO` and her actions `null and void`, raising questions about VOA's future role.
title: US judge voids 2025 actions taken by Kari Lake as Voice of America CEO, including job cuts

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Obama's eulogy as a tribute that delivers a critique—emphasizing Jackson's "immense gifts," calling Obama's lines "beautiful" and characterizing them as a "blistering rebuke" of Trump—while right-leaning coverage portrays the same remarks as inappropriate, using terms like "absolutely disgusting attack," "disrespect on steroids," and labeling the moment partisan, spotlighting Biden's "quip" to question fitness.
Center coverage focuses on "assaults on democracy" and reports the White House calling it a "total disgrace.
The divide hinges on whether politicizing a funeral is justified; both sides agree Obama spoke and criticized the administration, revealing broader cultural and ideological fault lines.
summary: Barack Obama warned the U.S. faces “daily assaults on democracy” while speaking at a memorial for civil rights leader Jesse Jackson.
The event in Chicago was attended by former leaders including Joe Biden, Bill Clinton, and former Vice President Kamala Harris.
Donald Trump did not attend the memorial due to scheduling, according to a White House official.
title: US faces assaults on democracy, Obama says at Jesse Jackson memorial

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the robotics head’s resignation as a "principled" stand against a "rushed" Pentagon deal, employing "unusually unsettling" language to highlight risks like "mass surveillance.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage portrays the contract as a "win," characterizing the departure as "backlash" rather than a moral crisis.
While centrist reports neutrally link the events, they occasionally label the deal "controversial.
The pivotal divide centers on "guardrails"; the left emphasizes ethical lapses, while the right de-emphasizes specific military risks.
Both perspectives agree the resignation occurred one week after the contract, reflecting broader tensions over AI’s role in warfare.
summary: On March 7, 2026, Caitlin Kalinowski, OpenAI 's head of robotics, resigned following OpenAI's deal last week to let the Department of Defense use its AI products on classified networks.

Citing governance concerns, Kalinowski wrote that OpenAI's leadership, including Sam Altman, rushed the announcement without defined guardrails, raising worries about surveillance and lethal autonomy.

During her 16 months at OpenAI, Kalinowski built the physical AI programme, including a San Francisco lab employing roughly 100 data collectors; she previously led hardware teams at Apple and Meta's Oculus.
The company also lost its most experienced hardware leader as ChatGPT uninstalls surged 295% and Anthropic's Claude climbed to No.1 in the U.S. App Store.

An OpenAI spokesperson told Business Insider that `We believe our agreement with the Pentagon creates a workable path for responsible national security uses of AI while making clear our red lines: no domestic surveillance and no autonomous weapons` after Anthropic talks collapsed.
title: OpenAI Robotics head resigns after deal with Pentagon

bias: Left outlets emphasize moral alarm and gender‑equality harm—framing the Ipsos finding with words like "shocking," "terrifying," "always" and "obey" to provoke outrage—while right coverage frames the cohort as "reactionary" or "backward" and introduces demographic causal claims to explain conservatism; center outlets focus on metrics and nuance.
Methodology and country‑level variation are pivotal dividing lines .
All sides cite the Ipsos numbers and express concern, reflecting deeper cultural and ideological fault lines.
summary: A study polling 23,000 people across 29 countries showed 31 percent of Generation Z men say a wife should always obey and 33 percent say a husband should have the final word.

Generation Z men, the youngest adult cohort, showed unexpectedly traditional expectations in the survey, with Kelly Beaver saying `This year’s survey shows us that we are witnessing perhaps a great re-negotiation of how both men and women inhabit gender roles in today’s society.`

Specific survey items show almost a quarter of Gen Z men oppose women appearing too independent, over one in five view caregiving as less masculine, and one in five said a `real woman` should never initiate sex.
Report authors emphasized the value of the study for debate, with Julia Gillard noting `many Gen Z men are imposing limits on women and themselves.
title: 31% of Gen Z men believe wife should 'obey her husband,' study finds
Hobbs Vetoes Bill for License Plates Memorializing Charlie Kirk
Judge skeptical of Pentagon's restrictions on press access

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story around official rebuttal and skepticism—foregrounding words like "denies" and "misrepresenting" and noting CENTCOM's denial—while right-leaning outlets amplify threat and outrage, using militarized, emotive terms such as "war," "conflict escalates," "captured forces," and accusatory phrases like "can't keep hiding truth" and "unprovoked American-Israeli aggression.
Rhetorically, the left favors contestation, the right stresses revelation and escalation, and the center highlights attribution and the absence of specifics .
Both sides nevertheless center Larijani's claim, underscoring broader geopolitical fault lines.
summary: On Saturday, Ali Larijani, head of Iran 's National Security Council, posted on the social media platform X that `It has been reported to me that several American soldiers have been taken prisoner`, alleging captures since the outbreak of war last week.
Larijani framed the report as concealed by Washington on X, while other accounts say Americans have been killed in action since the outbreak of war last week.
The US military quickly denied the claims with a statement online, and US Navy Captain Tim Hawkins said, `This is yet another clear example,` which US Central Command echoed to Al Jazeera Arabic.
With differing accounts, reports say Americans were killed in action while Larijani claims captures, and the situation remains a developing news story.
A US spokesman told Al Jazeera Arabic that the Iranian regime is `doing everything it can to peddle lies and deceive`, characterizing Larijani's claims as misleading.
title: Trump administration denies reports that Iran captured US soldiers

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the law as preventing falsehoods and protecting historical truth—using verbs like "forbid"/"ban" and pejoratives such as "cry" and "mind control" to cast Republican objections as performative—while right-leaning coverage frames the measure as partisan imposition, emphasizing coercive language like "require," "prevent," and labeling the mandated description as "insurrection" to portray censorship and hypocrisy; pivotal dividing lines are whether Jan. 6 is called "peaceful" versus "riot/insurrection" and bans on teaching "extensive election fraud," left outlets spotlight narrow vote margins and an expected gubernatorial signature, center outlets prefer legalistic "prohibits/falsehoods" language, and all sides nonetheless agree the legislature passed a bill restricting how Jan. 6 is taught, reflecting deeper ideological disputes over curriculum control and political narratives.
summary: On Thursday, the Virginia General Assembly approved HB333 to require public K-12 schools in Virginia to describe January 6, 2021, as an unprecedented, violent attack and ban portrayals of it as peaceful.
As the sponsor explained, HB333 was prompted by official messaging; Dan Helmer said it responds to a White House webpage and told CBS News, `The White House has tried to rewrite history`.

Passage prompted pushback from Republican legislators; HB333 passed Virginia House 53-47 and Senate 21-19, with Tom Garrett, Delegate , stating, `It tells us what we re not allowed to say, and it tells us what we must say.`

Gov. Abigail Spanberger is expected to sign HB333, making its instructional rules binding where local school boards adopt Jan. 6 programs, while similar bills are being discussed by the New York legislature.
Beyond classrooms, advocates cite nearly 1,600 clemency grants, over 140 officers injured, and five deaths, as Capitol memorial plaque installation highlights disputes over Jan. 6.
title: Virginia passes bill panning public schools from teaching that Jan. 6 was peaceful

summary: In a YouTube announcement, Cortical Labs said it demonstrated living human neurons playing Freedoom on the CL1 system and posted the demo and open-source code to YouTube and GitHub.

Building on neuron‑Pong demonstrations years ago with clusters of 800,000 to one million cells, Cortical Labs chose Doom as a practical, symbolic benchmark, completing it far faster than the 18 months for Pong.

Using a mapped stimulus‑response loop, the CL1 maps on‑screen elements to electrical stimuli that provoke neuronal spikes in 200,000 human neurons on a 59-electrode array managed by biOS, with Sean Cole training the neurons via Cortical Cloud and Python API in less than a week.
While performance trails humans, the system learns faster than traditional silicon-based machine learning and can improve with newer algorithms, experts say.
Under current lab conditions, the CL1 ships as a desktop or 30‑unit rack module, with each module costing around 1,000 dollars and consuming 850 to 1,000 watts per rack.
title: Human Brain Cells Learn to Play Doom on Biological Computer

bias: Left-Leaning coverage was de-emphasized in the analyses; right-leaning pieces frame Kiley’s shift to no party preference as a dramatic, blame-focused rescue—using words like "doomed," "hail Mary," personalizing the map as " Gavin Newsom ’s gerrymander," and relaying his "plague on democracy" rhetoric to emphasize victimhood and urgency.
Center-Leaning pieces offer a restrained take—saying he "tacked to the middle," calling the race a "longshot" and noting maps "blew up his boundaries," de-emphasizing theatrical blame.
The pivotal divides are personalization of responsibility and apocalyptic metaphors; both agree redistricting made his path harder, reflecting deeper partisan and institutional distrust.
summary: Rep. Kevin Kiley filed as a no party preference candidate on Friday to run in the newly drawn 6th District, which includes Roseville, Rocklin and Orangevale.
Gov. Gavin Newsom 's redistricting plan dismantled Kiley's old district into six new seats after voters approved Proposition 50 by a nearly 30-point margin.

Although he filed as no party preference, Kiley remains a registered Republican and a member of the GOP conference, and he proposed legislation in August 2025 to ban all mid-decade redistricting, which never received a floor vote.
Kiley did not say whether he would continue caucusing with Republicans, leaving House alignment uncertain as Rep. Darrell Issa announced Friday he would not run for reelection.

The June 2 primary will determine which two candidates advance to November, and Kiley faces a field including former State Sen. Richard Pan, Sacramento County District Attorney Thien Ho, West Sacramento Mayor Martha Guerrero, and Republican Christine Bish.
title: California Rep. Kevin Kiley Launches Independent Re-Election Bid After Redistricting

bias: Left coverage frames the appeals ruling as defending vulnerable migrants—calling TPS a "humanitarian program" and using "protections" and "cannot end"—while right outlets stress legal contest and security, noting a "divided" court and calling Haiti "gang-violence-stricken," portraying the same 350,000 beneficiaries in terms of homeland danger.
Center pieces de-emphasize advocacy, using legal terms like "upholds" and focusing on injunctions and DHS’s broader moves.
Pivotal divides are Haiti’s portrayal, the court split, and DHS scope; all agree the appeals court kept TPS intact for roughly 350,000 Haitians, reflecting humanitarian versus security priorities.
summary: On Friday, a 2-1 panel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit refused to pause a February 2 district court order, preserving protections for more than 350,000 Haitians.

Kristi Noem, Secretary of Homeland Security, moved in November to end Haiti's TPS as Haitian plaintiffs filed a class-action suit, and U.S. District Judge Ana C. Reyes blocked the termination in February.

The majority highlighted the lack of evidence of irreparable harm by the government, while Judges Florence Pan and Brad Garcia noted deported Haitians face violence amid a `collapsing rule of law `; Circuit Judge Justin Walker dissented, comparing the case to Venezuelan litigation.

The administration is expected to ask the U.S. Supreme Court to allow termination to proceed as Kristi Noem, Secretary of Homeland Security, testified on March 4, 2026, that conditions in Haiti do not prevent safe returns, though the program's future remains uncertain.

Haiti's TPS program began after the 2010 earthquake and was most recently renewed in July 2024 due to economic, security, political, and health crises; plaintiffs face devastating risks including deportation and violence if protections end.
title: Appeals court rules against Trump administration’s efforts to end protected status for Haitians

bias: Left outlets emphasize victimhood and state culpability, foregrounding the protester's injury with phrases like "broken arm" and partisan tags such as "MAGA Senator," framing the removal as repression; right outlets valorize the subject as "Marine veteran," stress resilience with "vows" and "suffers," and spotlight the scuffle and the senator "responds" to cast events as a chaotic brawl; center coverage is more action‑focused, using "forcibly removed" and "violent brawl" and de‑emphasizing explicit blame.
Pivotal divides are attribution of responsibility, mention of formal charges, and whose account is prioritized—yet all agree a veteran was ejected, injured, and intends to stay in the race.
summary: A Marine veteran and Green Party candidate of North Carolina was removed from a Senate subcommittee hearing on Capitol Hill, resisting efforts to escort him out.
Wearing his uniform to protest U.S. military assistance to Israel, McGinnis said he planned to ask lawmakers why Americans are being sent to war, prompting his presence at the hearing.
Capitol Police intervened as Senator Tim Sheehy sought to end the disruption and remove McGinnis, who forcefully resisted while Sheehy said he tried to `deescalate` the confrontation.
Authorities labeled the disruption unlawful while McGinnis framed his expulsion as fuel for his candidacy, saying he is only more determined after being forcibly removed.

The disruption underscores debate over U.S. military assistance to Israel and whether Americans should be sent to war, highlighting McGinnis's prior injury and candidacy amid House and Senate leaders discussing related war policy.
title: Ex-Marine forcibly removed from hearing vows to continue Senate campaign

bias: Left coverage frames the story as a residents' victory—using language like "win," "limiting," and even "barred"—emphasizing civilian harm and detailed tactics ; right coverage largely presents a neutral legal development—phrases like "obtained order"—de-emphasize operational detail and mirror terse wire copy.
Center pieces adopt moral-legal language that tempers emotion with institutional focus.

Pivotal divides center on mentions of children, additional munitions, and parallel lawsuits; both sides nonetheless agree a judge issued a preliminary injunction limiting tear gas, reflecting deeper community‑protection vs law ‑and‑order tensions.
summary: A federal judge ordered federal officers at the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement building in Portland to restrict their use of tear gas after a lawsuit by nearby tenants.
The judge acknowledged the order as 'an extraordinary remedy' but deemed the case 'extraordinary' due to the toxic chemicals poisoning a residential community.
The lawsuit argued that the use of chemical munitions violated residents' rights to life, liberty and property by sickening them and contaminating their apartments.
title: Portland residents near ICE building win court order limiting agents’ tear gas use

bias: Left outlets frame the installation as institutional failure—using phrases like "yearslong delay" and "gathering dust in a basement" and blaming House Republicans/Speaker Mike Johnson for politicized neglect—while right coverage valorizes law enforcement, stressing "honoring police officers" and "heroism" and portraying the plaque as overdue recognition.
Center pieces foreground logistics—"quietly" installed around 4 a.m. after a unanimous Senate vote—without strong blame.

Pivotal dividing lines include attribution of responsibility and the statutory deadline ; all sides nonetheless agree the plaque was placed roughly three years after Jan. 6, creating a shared anchor amid ideological frames.
summary: On Saturday, March 7, 2026, Architect of the Capitol staff installed a commemorative plaque inside the U.S. Capitol near the West Front in a predawn operation witnessed by a reporter about 4 a.m. after it sat in storage for years.

The 2022 federal law mandated the plaque's installation within a year, but delays occurred after House Speaker Mike Johnson's office said the statute was 'not implementable,' prompting legal action by officers.
The plaque lists the U.S. Capitol Police and the Metropolitan Police Department, accompanied by a sign with a QR code linking to a 45-page officer roster.

Critics noted the installation was done at 4 a.m. with no ceremony, while Daniel Hodges said the lawsuit will continue and Joe Morelle said, `Whether some people like it or not, the record of that day is now part of this building`.
Legal and prosecutorial outcomes underscore the event's long tail, with more than 1,500 charged, roughly 1,500 pardons, and more than 140 injured officers amid Justice Department dismissal efforts.
title: January 6 commemorative plaque appears in Capitol after years of delay

bias: Left-Leaning coverage portrays the intelligence report as a tool to "undercut" executive goals, using charged phrases like "pours cold water" and sarcastically referencing the "Dear Leader.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets focus on the "entrenched" nature of the "Iranian regime," employing hawkish terms like "topple" and "no kneeling" to frame the situation.
A key dividing line is the president’s demand for "unconditional surrender," which the left highlights while the right de-emphasizes.
Center-Leaning reports provide a "sobering assessment" of the "specter" of war .
Ultimately, all sides emphasize that 18 intelligence agencies agree a military assault is unlikely to succeed.
summary: Completed about a week before Feb. 28, a classified National Intelligence Council assessment found a large-scale U.S. military assault would be unlikely to remove the clerical and military leadership of Iran.

The National Intelligence Council found that Iran's succession mechanisms and internal procedures for continuity are designed to preserve authority, with the Assembly of Experts overseeing the replacement of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

Analysts noted the assessment draws on work from 18 U.S. intelligence agencies, and Suzanne Maloney, Iran scholar and vice president at the Brookings Institution, described the NIC assessment as credible.
The findings cast doubt on President Donald Trump 's plan to 'clean out' Iran's leadership, and the White House did not confirm if he reviewed the assessment before approving the military campaign.
With succession unfolding under bombardment, analysts note no new supreme leader has been announced and Iran's fragmented opposition seizing power is unlikely.
title: Classified US intel report warns large-scale war ‘unlikely’ to oust Iran’s regime: Washington Post

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the survey as a moral indictment of Americans—using terms like 'bad people' and a stark Canada contrast —emphasizing systemic social decay and religious divides, while right-leaning pieces spotlight numeric evidence , employ alarmist tones and questions, and use words like 'condemn,' 'judge harshest,' and 'divided and distrustful' to stress cultural decline and comparative country lists.
Center outlets de-emphasize moralizing language, preferring data-focused phrasing like 'especially likely' and highlighting partisan splits.
Pivotal flashpoints—Canada comparison, partisan and religious breakdowns, and judgments about behaviors such as affairs—drive divergent portrayals, though all cite the same 25-country survey.
summary: 53% of Americans described the morality and ethics of their fellow citizens as bad, the highest among 25 countries surveyed by Pew Research Center.
There was a partisan split, with 60% of Democrats and Democratic-leaning independents rating fellow Americans as morally and ethically bad compared to 46% of Republicans and Republican-leaners.
Pew noted it had not asked this specific question before, so it could not determine whether this was a long-term pattern or a newer shift in American views.
title: 53% of Americans View Fellow Citizens as Morally Bad, Highest Among 25 Countries in Pew Poll
Alibaba Says Its AI Agent Mined Crypto On Its Own During Training

bias: Left outlets frame the papal pick as a move "to manage relations with Trump," stressing a "veteran" envoy and flagging human‑rights concerns from his Philippines posting with phrases like "immigration crackdown" and "war in Iran"; right outlets nationalize and dramatize the choice—invoking an "American" tilt, talk of "reshaping" U.S.– Vatican dynamics, and calling a predecessor a "hatchet man" while warning about "radical migration policy.

Center pieces de‑emphasize polemic in favor of routine diplomacy; both sides nonetheless highlight the appointment, his UN résumé and strained ties, reflecting deeper ideological, cultural and geopolitical divides.
summary: Pope Leo XIV named Archbishop Gabriele Giordano Caccia apostolic nuncio to the United States, replacing Cardinal Christophe Pierre who is retiring at age 80.
Amid tensions over U.S. policy, Pope Leo XIV appointed a veteran diplomat to manage strained Holy See–U.S. ties and advise on bishop nominations.

Caccia brings diplomatic experience as the Holy See's permanent observer to the United Nations in New York since 2019, former nuncio to Lebanon and the Philippines, and assessor for general affairs at the Secretariat of State; he was born in Milan on Feb. 24, 1958.
Archbishop Paul S. Coakley welcomed Caccia with the U.S. bishops' `warmest welcome and our prayerful support`, praising Pierre for urging unity amid tensions.
Observers note Caccia inherits a complicated U.S. dossier and his U.N. background signals Vatican support for multilateral diplomacy, while he must be discreet and unifying, `in the image of Leo`.
title: Pope names veteran Vatican diplomat as ambassador to the US to manage relations with Trump
Proton Mail Shared User Payment Data That Helped FBI Identify Protest Organizer

bias: Left coverage frames the White House meeting as proof of profiteering and escalation—using phrases like "stand to profit off" and "military‑industrial complex" and warnings about "scariest moments"—while right outlets celebrate decisive action, deploying heroic diction such as "rallies," "defense titans," and upbeat uses of "quadruple" and "as rapidly as possible" to assure readiness amid a war that "rages.
Center coverage de‑emphasizes moralizing, attributing claims to the president and noting caveats about timing and verification.

Flashpoints include how to read the "quadruple" pledge and Lockheed's claim—profit motive versus patriotic replenishment—yet all agree a high‑level meeting occurred and production increases were announced, reflecting deeper ideological splits over militarism and corporate influence.
summary: At the White House on Friday, President Donald Trump announced CEOs agreed to quadruple production of the 'Exquisite Class' weaponry.
Amid the first full week of the Iran war, the White House faced scrutiny over low munitions supplies, but Karoline Leavitt, White House Press Secretary, said the meeting was scheduled weeks ago.

According to Trump, CEOs from BAE Systems, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, RTX, Boeing, Honeywell Aerospace, L3Harris Technologies' Missile Solutions, and U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth attended, and `Expansion began three months prior to the meeting, and Plants and Production of many of these Weapons are already under way`.
In immediate terms, the White House framed the effort as addressing supply gaps, linking increased production to sustaining operations such as `Operation Epic Fury` and to report shortfalls.
The 'Exquisite Class' weaponry label was left undefined by Trump, with the defense industry usage describing it as a one-of-a-kind technology, even as production expands.
title: Trump says defense contractors agree to quadruple production of ‘exquisite class’ weapons

bias: Left coverage emphasizes a celebratory, humanistic victory—using phrases like "save‑the‑Earth test," "Take the win!" and claims that "humanity has permanently signed its name"—whereas right coverage frames the mission in security/militaristic terms—"smash into," "doomsday," "in win for future missions"—stressing existential stakes and decisive action.
Rhetorically, left uses uplifting symbolism; right uses alarmist, triumphalist language.
Pivotal divides include existential framing versus technical quantification , which center outlets foreground with measured detail.
Both sides nonetheless highlight planetary‑defense relevance, reflecting deeper cultural and geopolitical priorities.
summary: Published March 6 in Science Advances, researchers reported the Didymos–Dimorphos system's velocity decreased by roughly 11.7 micrometers per second after the DART impact.
NASA's DART spacecraft rammed Dimorphos in September 2022, and impact ejecta acted like an extra rocket plume, roughly doubling the momentum transfer with a beta parameter near two.
Using 22 stellar occultations and nearly 6,000 ground-based astrometric measurements, researchers detected the orbit shrank by about 1,200 feet and 0.15 seconds.

Despite the tiny change, teams emphasize Earth remains safely out of the asteroids' path, and the study advances planetary-defense planning with European Space Agency's Hera spacecraft arriving later this year.

Questions remain about the impact's precise contributions, and researchers say Hera spacecraft's survey later this year will clarify Dimorphos' post-impact shape and mass, while scientists note small early impulses can accumulate over years.
title: NASA's DART Impact Altered Asteroid's Orbit Around the Sun, Study Finds

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Israel’s military renewal as an "assault," employing terms like "vows" and "many surprises" to portray leadership as "menacing" or "provocative.
Conversely, right-leaning perspectives de-emphasize aggression, instead characterizing the shift as an "intensification" of conflict.
While the left highlights the IDF’s rhetoric regarding "terrorist elements," center-leaning coverage provides unique geographic specificity, noting strikes reached "Beirut.
Despite these framing differences, all sides emphasize Netanyahu’s "many surprises" and the targeting of Quds Force commanders.
These portrayals reflect a fundamental divide between viewing military actions as offensive "assaults" versus strategic, necessary escalations.
summary: On Sunday, Israel renewed its assault on southern Lebanon, striking military targets including commanders of the Lebanese branch of Iran's Revolutionary Guards' Quds Force after Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu promised `many surprises`.
On Saturday, an attack hit an oil storage facility south of Tehran, sending pillars of fire visible at night, with state media blaming `an attack from the US and the Zionist regime`.
After Saturday's strikes, officials said the death toll rose with eight killed in southern Lebanon, four in a Beirut hotel, and at least 1,230 deaths in Iran.

Conflicting signals from Iran's leadership could complicate diplomacy, as Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologised on Saturday while hard-line judiciary chief Gholam Hossein Mohseni-Ejei signalled no strategy change.

With US strikes launched from regional bases and vessels, officials said operations would continue as Iran's UN mission suggested strikes may have resulted from interception by US electronic defence systems.
title: Israel Renews Assault on Lebanon After Netanyahu Promises 'Many Surprises' in Next Phase of War

bias: Left-Leaning coverage humanizes Rama Duwaji with celebratory terms like "love of his life" and "First Lady," foregrounding her art and public role, while right-leaning pieces emphasize alleged social‑media likes and moral culpability using charged words like "liked," "mass hoax," "rapes," and "under fire"; the Oct. 7 sexual‑violence allegation and Mamdani’s reassurances to Jewish New Yorkers form pivotal dividing lines that produce very different tones.

Center coverage is de-emphasized, offering neither strong profile nor scandal framing, and both sides nonetheless note her visibility at the swearing‑in, reflecting broader cultural and ideological divides.
summary: During a news conference on Sunday, Mamdani said he defended his wife, Rama Duwaji, saying she is a private person.
Recent coverage highlights reports resurfaced in the past days showing Duwaji liked posts tied to the Oct. 7, 2023 attack and a February 2024 Instagram post dismissing a New York Times probe.
Her rising public profile includes a photoshoot cover for The Cut and marriage in early 2025 at the City Clerk's office, shared by Mamdani.
Mamdani's communications representatives said he condemns Hamas and called October 7th a horrific war crime, while the mayor warned right-wing trolls aim to make the race about his wife.
With political stakes high, Mamdani defends his wife amid social media scrutiny over posts liking controversial content, as he faces citywide political challenges at the start of 2026.
title: Mamdani’s Wife Rama Duwaji Liked Post Calling Oct. 7 Hamas Rapes of Israelis a ‘Mass Hoax’: Report

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames detainees as "pro‑Palestinian activists" organizing a "humanitarian flotilla" and emphasizes that authorities "detained" them—conveying solidarity and rights-focused concern—while right-leaning pieces de-emphasize that portrayal, calling the mission a "phantom humanitarian mission" and flagging "irregularities," adopting a skeptical, accusatory tone.
Left uses legitimizing labels and empathetic diction; right uses delegitimizing, judgmental language.
Pivotal dividing lines are the flotilla's legitimacy, organizers' stated aims, and the reported financial inquiry.
Center reports use neutral verbs and affiliation labels.
Both sides agree detentions occurred and tensions mounted, reflecting deeper ideological splits over activism versus security.
summary: On Friday, Tunisian authorities detained several pro-Palestinian activists amid a probe by Tunisia 's National Guard financial crimes unit into donation funds for a planned flotilla.
Authorities banned a planned event Thursday in Tunis expected to host Thiago Ávila, Greta Thunberg and Seif Abu Koshk, while earlier this week security forces blocked a ceremony at Sidi Bou Said.
Organizers say the new flotilla will include more than 1,000 activists and sail from Spain, Tunisia and Italy with a land convoy expected to attract thousands more.

Global Sumud Flotilla organizers accused authorities of targeting activists and called for their immediate release; Tunisian authorities did not comment, and Ávila was detained Friday at Brussels Airport.

The move occurs amid Gaza's ongoing humanitarian crisis as Israel suspended more than two dozen humanitarian organizations and the United Nations reports large aid stockpiles since the October ceasefire.
title: Tunisian authorities detain pro-Palestinian activists preparing new Gaza aid flotilla
Federal funding for people in poverty heading to Missouri anti-abortion centers

bias: Right-Leaning coverage frames the story as celebratory, highlighting in‑party figures and using charged phrases like "tops," "highest," "Place Their Faith," and accusations that the "media" "trashes" him daily or that a "fake news" narrative is "decimated.
Center coverage instead emphasizes national decline and foreign‑policy impact, using words like "hits new low" and "souring" tied to Iran strikes.
Left-Leaning treatment is de‑emphasized here.
Both sides, however, rely on the same polls —revealing a split between partisan loyalty narratives and concern about broader public reaction.
summary: On Tuesday, the Economist/YouGov poll found 38% approval and 59% disapproval among 1,515 U.S. adults surveyed Feb. 27–March 2 with a 3.4 margin of error.

Poll respondents pointed to the war with Iran when citing the decline, with 59% saying their views worsened due to recent foreign interventions like 'Operation Midnight Hammer' and 'Operation Epic Fury'.
Despite the dip overall, Republicans strongly back President Donald Trump , with 86% approval and 53% 'strongly approve,' according to recent polls and analyst Harry Enten on March 5.
With 51% saying they strongly disapprove, political analysts warn that approval levels could influence the 2026 midterms and campaign strategies, as Grant Davis Reeher noted.
Across pollsters, approval trends indicate a decline, with VoteHub, RealClearPolling and The New York Times aggregator reporting lower approval numbers recently, nearing minus-21 approval levels.
title: Economist/YouGov poll: Trump approval hits second-term low at 38%

bias: Left outlets frame the post as an authoritarian " transgender edict" and an embarrassing backtrack—using words like "backlash," "quietly deleted," "gender‑affirming surgery" and "trans kids" to evoke outrage—while right outlets portray the change as a corrective "revision," stressing "mutilation" and parental consent, using "revises" to signal clarification.
The pivotal divides are the use of "mutilation" and the emphasis on parental consent, which produce starkly different readings ; center outlets take a measured "faced Republican criticism" tone.
Both agree the post was altered after conservative criticism, reflecting deeper cultural and ideological splits.
summary: Thursday morning, Trump posted on Truth Social promoting the SAVE America Act that included a parental-consent clause for gender-affirming surgery for minors, then deleted the post later that day.

Prominent right-wing commentators reacted swiftly as conservative commentators and activists blasted the parental-consent wording Thursday, with Marjorie Taylor Greene and Matt Walsh leading criticism.

At 10:39 a.m. ET, the five-point Truth Social post appeared bundling voting restrictions with an anti-trans point, then was edited hours later after criticism from conservatives like Greene and Walsh.
Conservative allies said the president was forced to alter the post amid huge backlash, highlighting GOP tensions over anti-trans policy and the Trump administration's broader LGBTQ + policy actions.

In January, Trump signed Protecting Children From Chemical And Surgical Mutilation, restricting care for trans youth under 19, while the Protect Children’s Innocence Act would criminalize care for under-18s and WPATH notes surgeries for minors are rare and require age of majority.
title: Trump Faces Republican Criticism Before Change To Transgender Children Post
